content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Mr. Ignacio Velazquez 
Mayor 
375 Fifth Street 
Hollister, CA 95023-3876 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-105 
Dear Mayor Velazquez: 
May 26, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
May you participate in a decision regarding a vacant property currently owned by the City's 
Successor Agency (formerly the City of Hollister Redevelopment Agency) and which is located 
within 500 feet of two properties you own? 
CONCLUSION 
You have a conflict of interest in decisions regarding vacant property which is located 
within 500 feet of two properties you own. The public generally exception would not apply. 
FACTS 
The City of Hollister has been discussing the future use or potential development and sale of 
a vacant lot. The lot is within 500 feet of two properties you own. The current proposal for the 
vacant lot includes a mixed use concept with sixteen apartments and retail on the lower floor. 
• A commercial building adjacent to the lot that is used entirely as a banquet facility for 
private functions such as weddings. You own the building and the business that operates the 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. AH statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-105 
Page No. 2 
banquet facility. You do not anticipate any increase or decrease of business or the building's 
value as a result of the future use of the vacant lot next door. 
• A commercial office building within 500 feet of the lot. This building is used as office rental 
space on the upper two floors and retail space on the lower floor. You currently have your 
office located on the third and first floors of the office building. You do not anticipate any 
increase or decrease of business or building value as a result of the proposals for the vacant 
lot. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a government decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a government decision on a public official's interests is material. The interests that apply 
here are: 
• A business entity in which you have a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more 
(Section 87103(a)); or in which you are a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• Real property in which you have a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 
87103(b).) 
• Any person who is a source of income (including promised income) aggregating $500 or 
more within 12 months prior to the decision.2 (Section 87103(c).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in 
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party 
in, or subject of, a government decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) From the facts provided, it does not 
appear that your financial interests are explicitly involved in a decision on the vacant lot. For 
interests that are not explicitly involved, as here, the effect need not be likely to be considered 
reasonably foreseeable. "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation I 8701 (b ).) 
Business Interests: There are several factors to consider to detennine if a foreseeable effect 
on a business entity would be material. If a business entity is not directly involved in the decision 
regarding the project, as here, then typically the inqu{ry is whether a "prudent person with sufficient 
infonnation would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
2 You have not provided any facts regarding your sources of income (lessees) for use to analyze. Therefore, we 
do not further analyze this interest. However, you should be mindful that a person who rents property adjacent to the lot 
could be foreseeably affected by the development depending on the nature of the business. Ifit appears that any of your 
sources of income will be impacted, you should contact us for further advice. 


File No. A-16-105 
Page No. 3 
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a 
privately-held business entity." (Regulation 18702.1 (b ).) 
To determine whether a foreseeable effect is material, factors to consider include whether 
the decision would: 
"(I) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an 
activity in which the business entity is engaged; 
"(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which 
the business entity is engaged; 
"(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the 
business entity supplies; 
"(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as 
redevelopment projects, traffidroad improvements, or parking changes that may 
affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business 
entity receives; 
"(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or 
other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity 
draws from the location of the project; or 
"(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability 
of the business entity." (Regulation 18702.1 (b )( 1 )-( 6).) 
The decisions here involve potentially developing the vacant lot with sixteen apartments and retail 
on the lower floor. Nothing in your facts suggests that Commissioner Evans' architectural business 
will be materially affected. 
Real Properly Interests: For real property not explicitly involved in a decision and pertinent 
to your facts, a foreseeable effect is material if the decision: 
"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
*** 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l0) and (12).) 


File No. A-16-105 
Page No. 4 
Based on the limited facts you provided, it would appear that the conversion of a currently vacant 
lot to sixteen apartments and retail on the lower floor could change the character of the parcel of 
real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air 
quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property 
parcel in which the official has a financial interest. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 0).) Moreover, having 
two commercial properties in such close proximity to the vacant lot would cause a reasonably 
prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the 
governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence 
the market value of your real property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)( 12).) Thus, the effect of the 
decision will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on your interests and you may not 
make, participate in making, or influence the decision. 
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a city 
councilmember) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: 
(1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest 
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) 
recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on 
the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) 
Public Generally Exception 
Regulation 18703 provides: 
"(a) General Rule. A governmental decision's financial effect on a public 
official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is 
affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to 
the effect on the significant segment. 3 
A significant segment of the public (as pertinent to your facts) is at least 25 
percent of: 
"All businesses or non-profit entities within the official's jurisdiction; 
A unique effect on a public official's financial interest (as pertinent to your facts) 
includes a disproportionate effect on: 
"(l) The development potential or use of the official's real property or ort 
the income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the 
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
3 Regulation I 8703(e) provides specific rules for special circumstances, none of which apply to your facts. 


File No. A-16-105 
Page No. 5 
"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting 
from the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
"(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting 
from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property 
size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
"(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property 
if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate 
family." 
Based on your facts, it appears unlikely you would meet the 25% threshold required by the 
exception. Even if 25% of the businesses in the city (or your election district) were in the same 
general proximity to the project as your own businesses, your interests would likely be affected 
uniquely based on your ownership of multiple interests around the site. Thus, we conclude the 
public generally exception would not apply. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
. Wallace 
Assi tant General Counsel 
--Ê,.,1=;al Division

within 500 feet of two properties you own?

QUESTION: May you participate in a decision regarding a vacant property currently owned by the City's 
Successor Agency (formerly the City of Hollister Redevelopment Agency) and which is located 
within 500 feet of two properties you own?

CONCLUSION: You have a conflict of interest in decisions regarding vacant property which is located 
within 500 feet of two properties you own. The public generally exception would not apply.
